coredata.dc:title: A facile strategy to realize metal-free room-temperature phosphorescence by construct nitrogen doped carbon dots-based nanocomposite
coredata.dc:creator: Zan, Minghui
An, Shuai
Jia, Mingzheng
Cao, Lei
Li, Li
Ge, Mingfeng
Wu, Zaihui
Mei, Qian
Dong, Wen-Fei
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention in constructing metal-free phosphorescence materials, but it is still a huge challenge to develop a material with long lifetime and phosphorescence emission in aqueous environments. In this work, we designed and synthesized a universal approach to produce N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with efficient room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) through simple heating treatment. Then, we prepared a kind of highly efficient N-CDs/MA nanocomposite phosphorescence materials by synthesized through N-CDs mixed with melamine. Excitingly, the characterizations indicate that the nanocomposite materials not only have an ultralong phosphorescence lifetime in the solid state, but also exhibit blue-green RTP in aqueous medium. In addition, based on their unusual RTP features, the obtained RTP materials can be successfully applied in ion determination and shows great potentials in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Room-temperature phosphorescence
Melamine
Information encryption

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation procedure of N-CDs and MA nanocomposite.

(a) TEM image, (b) size distribution, (c) FT-IR spectra of N-CDs. (d) XPS survey spectra and enlarged parts for (e) C 1 s, (f) N1s, (g) O 1 s of N-CDs. (h) Excitation dependence PL spectra of N-CDs aqueous solution.

(a) Phosphorescence decay spectra of the N-CDs powder. (b) Time-resolved phosphorescence spectra of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite powder. (c) Phosphorescence lifetime decay spectra of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite in the presence of water. (d) The proposed schematic illustration of the molecular interactions between the N-CDs, MA and water molecules.

(a) Phosphorescence spectra of N-CDs/MA suspension after incubation with different concentrations of Fe3+. (b) The liner plot of (F0−F)/F and the concentrations Fe3+. (c) Fluorescence spectra of N-CDs/MA suspension after addition of various concentrations of Fe3+. (d) The relationship between the fluorescence intensity variation and the Fe3+ concentration.

Security protection applications. (a) The digital photos of QD code made from N-CDs/MA nanocomposite material under daylight, 365 nm UV lamp ON and OFF, respectively. (b) Digital photos of lifetime-encoding for information encryption.
Summarized RTP properties of different materials in solid state or water environment.

head.ce:title: A facile strategy to realize metal-free room-temperature phosphorescence by construct nitrogen doped carbon dots-based nanocomposite

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Bio-Medical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215163, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Bio-Medical Diagnostics
Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: CAS Key Laboratory of Bio-Medical Diagnostics, Suzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Suzhou, Jiangsu 215163, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhengzhou Institute of Biomedical Engineering and Technology, Zhengzhou, Henan 450001, China

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for the formation procedure of N-CDs and MA nanocomposite and their applications.

Carbon dots (CDs) have received much attention in constructing metal-free phosphorescence materials, but it is still a huge challenge to develop a material with long lifetime and phosphorescence emission in aqueous environments. In this work, we designed and synthesized a universal approach to produce N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) with efficient room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) through simple heating treatment. Then, we prepared a kind of highly efficient N-CDs/MA nanocomposite phosphorescence materials by synthesized through N-CDs mixed with melamine. Excitingly, the characterizations indicate that the nanocomposite materials not only have an ultralong phosphorescence lifetime in the solid state, but also exhibit blue-green RTP in aqueous medium. In addition, based on their unusual RTP features, the obtained RTP materials can be successfully applied in ion determination and shows great potentials in the fields of anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

ce:section.ce:para: Room-temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials have aroused an increasing attention for applications in the fields of information storage, optical imaging, chemical sensors and anti-counterfeiting due to their long-lived luminescence and unique singlet–triplet process [1–10]. Until very recently, the efficient RTP from pure organic compounds and organometallic materials containing rare-earth elements or noble metals has been developed in the air compatibility [11–13]. In this respect, although these RTP materials offer the advantages of exhibit afterglow lifetimes of minutes to hours, they are involve complicated preparation procedure, extremely sensitive to oxygen, high toxicity and cost, which constitutes many of the drawbacks for limit their application range [14–17]. Therefore, it is highly desirable to development of new types of nontoxic and new metal-free efficient RTP materials.

Carbon dots (CDs), as a new type of luminescent nanomaterials, have drawn increasing attention in various fields owing to their superior optical properties, facile synthesis, low toxicity and biocompatibility [18–31]. Recently, unique RTP phenomena based on CDs has been discovered has greatly enlarged their application prospects. Since 2013, room temperature phosphorescence from CDs in polyvinyl alcohol matrix was firstly reported, and its phosphorescent lifetime to be 380 ms [32]. Subsequently, Tao et al. reported blue-green RTP from the polymer carbon dots without the need for adopting of complicated matrix compositing [33]; Jiang et al. demonstrated CDs-based powders exhibit the RTP lifetime to be 1.46 s via a microwave-assisted method [34]; Long et al. reported nitrogen and fluorine codoped CDs with blue-green RTP for information encryption and decryption [35]; Li et al. designed an effective and universal approach to activate RTP of CDs by one-step heating treatment of CDs with boric acid [36]. However, these reported RTP emission based on CDs only in their dry state. Phosphorescence intensity would suffer significantly quenching in the presence of water ascribing to the disruption of hydrogen bonds and the existence of dissolved oxygen. These disadvantageous factors seriously limit the aqueous application of RTP materials, including the chemical sensors and bioimaging.

In this paper, we put forward a facile route to realize RTP emission by utilizing N-CDs and MA to construct N-CDs/MA nanocomposite in the presence of water (Fig. 1 ). First, we report a novel nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) obtained by hydrothermal treatment, which exhibits good water-dispersibility and blue emission in solution under UV (365 nm) lamp irradiation. Impressively, the N-CDs powders possess the intriguing features of ultralong phosphorescence lifetime of up to 631.8 ms under ambient conditions. Then, we prepared a kind of highly efficient N-CDs/MA nanocomposite phosphorescence materials by synthesized through N-CDs mixed with melamine in aqueous solution under heating conditions. To our surprise, the nanocomposite RTP materials not only have an ultralong phosphorescence lifetime of 0.64 s in the solid state, but also exhibit a phosphorescence lifetime of 499.8 ms with blue-green RTP in aqueous medium, which is an amazing result higher than most of the reported metal-free RTP materials so far. Moreover, the obtained N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension can be utilized to Fe3+ detection based on dual emission (fluorescence and phosphorescence) feature. Benefiting from these outstanding RTP performances, the as-prepared RTP materials also promising in information encryption and anti-counterfeiting with a higher level of security.

Urea, polyacrylic acid (Mw = 3000 g/mol) (PAA), melamine, and quinine sulfate were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Company. All the chemicals were analytically pure and used without additional purification. Deionized water (18.2 MΩ·cm resistivity at 25 °C) prepared by a Milli-Q water system was used throughout the experiments.

UV–visible absorption spectra were recorded on Agilent Cary 300 Scan UV–vis absorption spectrophotometer. Photoluminescence (PL), phosphorescence excitation and emission spectra were measured using Hitachi F-4600 spectrophotometer under ambient conditions. The time-resolved emission spectra and the lifetime were measured by Edinburgh FLS1000 fluorescence spectrophotometer equipped with xenon arc lamp (Xe900), a microsecond flash-lamp (μF900), and a nanosecond hydrogen flash-lamp (nF920), respectively. The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) was performed on a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope. The samples solutions (15 μL) were dropped onto a copper TEM grid and then dried overnight. Dynamic laser light scattering (DLS) instrument was performed on a Malvern Zetasizer (Nano-ZS90). Fourier transform inftared (FT-IR) spectra were acquired from an Agilent Cary 660 FT-IR spectrometer. X-Ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) measurements were carried out on an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (AXIS ULTRA DLD). The average lifetimes was calculated according to the following equation:
t
ave
=
A 1
τ 1 2
+
A 2
τ 2 2
/
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2
Where A1 and A2 represent the relative weights of time-resolved fluorescence decay lifetimes τ1 and τ2, respectively.

The N-CDs was prepared by hydrothermal method. Briefly, urea (0.5 g) and PAA (0.25 g) were dissolved in DI water (30 mL) and mixed uniformly. Then, the clear solution was transferred into a 50 mL Teflon liner stainless steel autoclave and heated at 200 °C for 10 h. The autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution was subjected to dialysis (MWCO: 3000 Da) against deionized water for a week to remove small molecules. Finally, solid N-CDs was obtained as a pale yellow power via freeze-drying.

N-CDs (10 mg) was first diluted with 10 mL of DI water and then mixed with melamine (1 g) under constant stirring in an oil bath at 150 °C for 2 h. After cooling to room temperature, the mixtures were centrifuged to remove agglomerated particles, and then the solutions were freeze-dried to obtain powdered N-CDs/MA nanocomposite. Finally, the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite powders dispersed in water for further characterization and use.

Measurement of Fe3+ ions using the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite based fluorescent was executed through following procedures. In brief, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (20 μL) solutions were added separately to 1 mL of N-CDs/MA nanocomposite (0.15 g/mL) solutions, and then equilibrated for 2 min. In addition, the fluorescence intensity of the mixture were measured at excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The fluorescence behavior of N-CDs/MA nanocomposite with other metal ions solutions was also investigated in a similar way at room temperature.

For the detection of Fe3+ ions, different concentrations of Fe3+ ions (20 μL) solutions were added separately to 1 mL of N-CDs/MA nanocomposite (0.15 g/mL) solutions, and then equilibrated for 2 min. The phosphorescence intensity of the mixture were measured at excitation wavelength of 340 nm. The sensitivity and selectivity measurements were conducted in triplicate at room temperature.

The morphology of N-CDs was firstly characterized using dynamic laser light scattering (DLS) and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (Fig. 2 a and b). According to DLS images, the hydrodynamic diameter of as-prepared carbon dots is 6.5 nm. As shown in Fig. 2a, a TEM image demonstrates the average size of the N-CDs is 4.5 nm, with a narrow size well-dispersed in the range of 3 to 8 nm, which is in good agreement with the DLS characterization results.

The chemical compositions and surface groups of the N-CDs were investigated by Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). As seen in Fig. 2c, the N-CDs show broad FT-IR absorptions at ∼ 2500–3400 cm−1, which are attributed to the stretching vibrations of amino (NH2) and hydroxyl (–OH). Meanwhile, the characteristic peaks at 1650, 1527 and 1385 cm−1 could be attributed to C = O, N–H and C = N stretching vibrations, respectively. The results of FT-IR spectra confirmed that the N-CDs were functionalized by carboxyl (–COOH), amino groups (–NH2) and hydroxyl (–OH), which explains the high solubility of the as-prepared carbon dots in water.

The surface functional groups of the produced N-CDs were measured by XPS. Fig. 2d showed survey spectra of the carbon dots, there were mainly contain three typical peaks at 531.5, 397.8 and 286.0 eV for C 1 s, N 1 s and O 1 s, respectively. Meanwhile, the concentrations of C, N and O elements were 50.2%, 40.7% and 9.1%, respectively, demonstrating relatively high doped nitrogen atoms in the carbon dots. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1 s (Fig. 2e) demonstrates the existence of C = O (287.4 eV), C-O/C-N (285.2 eV) and C–C/C = C (284.5 eV). The XPS N 1 s spectra (Fig. 2f) show two peaks at 398.8 eV and 398.1 eV, which are assigned to the N–H and C-N groups, respectively. The XPS O 1 s spectrum (Fig. 2g) exhibits two peaks at binding energy of 533.2 eV and 531.4 eV that attributed to C-O and C = O bands, respectively. Taken together, the XPS results are agreement with our FT-IR data.

To study the photophysical properties (absorption and fluorescence) of N-CDs, the UV–vis and photoluminescence spectra of as-prepared N-CDs were fully investigated. As shown in Figure S1, the N-CDs exhibit typical absorption peak at 250 nm is due to π-π* transitions of C = C bonds. Furthermore, a broad band from 250 to ∼ 300 nm may be correspond to the n-π* transition of the C = N bond or C-N bond. The as-prepared N-CDs exhibited a maximum emission at 395 nm at the optimal excitation wavelength of 330 nm, and the carbon dots solution emits bright blue fluorescence under UV light (365 nm). As seen in Fig. 2h, similar to most luminescent carbon dots, the N-CDs displayed the excitation-dependent fluorescence behavior, which is due to different sizes distribution, surface states and edge defects of N-CDs. With the increase of excited FL wavelength from 300 nm to 400 nm, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs exhibited a maximum peak locked at 395 nm. In addition, the average fluorescence lifetime (Figure S2 and Table S1) of the N-CDs was ∼ 4.19 ns. Such a short fluorescence lifetime also indicates the radioactive recombination processes in the surface states and the core states was responsible for the photoluminescent mechanism.

We note that for the aqueous of N-CDs no phosphorescence signal can be detected at room temperature. More interestingly, an efficient RTP that lasts several seconds can be easily observed from N-CDs powders when removed the UV light under ambient conditions (Figure S3). Meanwhile, the color of the N-CDs powders change from blue to green. As expected, the phosphorescence decay spectrum of N-CDs powders (Fig. 3 a) can be fitted into a multi-exponential function, and the average lifetime is calculated to be 631.8 ms under ambient conditions (detailed results of fitting are summarized in Table S2). Based on the above analysis and reported results [33], a possible mechanism for the activation of room temperature phosphorescence from N-CDs is proposed as followed. First of all, The multiple lifetimes of the phosphorescence suggest a variety of emission species, which could be attributed to different chemical environments on the surface of N-CDs for aromatic carbonyls such as C = N and C = O bands. Secondly, the rigidity of hydrogen bonding may play an important role in rigidifying the C = O bonds on the surface of nitrogen doped carbon dots, which restricting the molecular motions to minimize the non-radiative transition for RTP emission. The absolute fluorescence quantum yield of N-CDs is measured to be 31.5% in aqueous solution and as high as 23.1% in solid powders state (for both fluorescence and phosphorescence), which proving the significant contribution of nitrogen to photoluminescence.

Astonishingly, an ultralong afterglow emission was readily observed from the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite aqueous solution when N-CDs mixed with melamine in aqueous solution under heating conditions. As seen in Figure S4, the CDs/MA nanocomposite also shows afterglow in an aqueous environment and is visible to the unaided eye. The corresponding afterglow lifetime of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite was 0.64 s in the solid state (Fig. 3b). Compared with the lifetime values of solid-state N-CDs, there are no significant differences were detected in Table S2. Furthermore, we monitored the RTP lifetime decay curves of N-CDs/MA nanocomposite in the presence of water. As anticipated, the average lifetime of water-RTP was 499.8 ms (Fig. 3c). Compared with other RTP based on CDs, the as-prepared CDs/MA nanocomposite shows RTP from both solid state and even in water environment (Table 1 ) [33,37–40]. As demonstrated in the Figure S5, the phosphorescence and fluorescence spectrum of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite in aqueous medium were investigated, where an optimal excitation wavelength is 340 nm and 330 nm. The chemical structure of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite was investigated by FT-IR. In the FT-IR spectrum of the nanocomposite (Figure S6), the characteristic absorption peaks of the as-manufactured nanocomposite still appeared compared with that of the originated N-CDs.

We deduce that a possible mechanism for the water-RTP emission is presented in Fig. 3d. Melamine is a unique organic base consisting of three cyanamide with a 1,3,5-triazine skeleton, three amine groups and three acceptor locations, offering a large amount of hydrogen bonding possibilities. In the presence of water molecules, the melamine molecules surfaces can strongly absorb a layer of highly ordered water molecules. This type of water exhibits unique structural properties including restricted solubility and mobility, difficult separation, and constructs strong hydrogen-bonded (N-H···O and O-H···O) networks between the N-CDs and MA particles, which not only can help to rigidify the C = O bonds on the surface of N-CDs but also makes great contributions to hinder molecular motions for suppressing non-radiative transition of singlet and triplet excitons, resulting in the ultralong phosphorescence at room temperature.

Recently, a great variety of CDs were synthesized for ion detection via fluorescence procedures. However, compared with fluorescence sensors, phosphorescence detection can effectively minimize the autofluorescence background and scattering light from real samples. Inspired by the above-mentioned advantage, we expect to develop the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension could be used as a sensor to discriminate iron ions in the water. The phosphorescence and fluorescence of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite can be seen distinctly quenched by Fe3+ ions. However, there is displayed little interference even if in the presence of other excesses metal ions (Figure S7). Therefore, these results reveal that the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite are highly selective probes for Fe3+ ions over the other ions. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a and b, the phosphorescence of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite was significantly decreased with Fe3+ concentration in the range of 0 to 2 mM. The regression equation can be described as:
y = 0.139 + 0.337 x
The correlation coefficient (R2 ) is 0.996, and the limit of detection (LOD) for the Fe3+ is 20.3 μM (Signal to noise is 3).

Meanwhile, as shown in Fig. 4c and 4d, when the concentration of Fe3+ ions changed from 0 to 1 mM, the fluorescence intensity decreased obviously. There is a linear dependence (Regression coefficient is 0.9937) between (F0−F)/F and the concentration of Fe3+ in the range of 0–1 mM. Additionally, the limit of detection was also evaluated to be 28.6 μM (S/N = 3). Compared the previous reported probes in sensing Fe3+ ions (Table S3), the selectivity and sensitivity of this work showed better or comparable in both phosphorescence and fluorescence manners. Based on the consequences of characterization, we speculate that the quenching mechanism of phosphorescence and fluorescence would rely on the special coordination interaction between Fe3+ ions and oxygen groups (such as hydroxyl, amine and carboxyl groups) on the surface of the N-CDs. This non-luminescent complex changes the original N-CDs energy level structure, finally give rise to the non-radiative recombination and leading phosphorescence and FL quenching of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension. To further assess the quenching mechanism, we investigated the fluorescence and RTP lifetimes of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension. As shown in Figure S8 and S9, the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension average fluorescence lifetimes are almost no change before and after the coordination of Fe3+ ions (3.84 ns and 3.62 ns, respectively), as did the RTP lifetime of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension (499.8 ms and 473.5 ms, respectively). The above results clearly indicated that RTP quenching of the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite suspension induced by Fe3+ ions can be attributed to a static quenching effect. Therefore, phosphorescence and fluorescence dual independent results can give more precise and responsible measurements, and the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite system provides a new and general strategy for the detection of Fe3+ ions.

Inspired by the superior feature of the obtained ultralong RTP materials, their potential application in information identification and data encryption were demonstrated. As illustrated in Fig. 5 a, a monochromic two-dimensional code was printed onto a piece of filter paper (non-fluorescent), the gray part of the QR code was the ground obtained N-CDs/MA nanocomposite material. After complete drying, upon UV irradiation, the printed pattern become luminescent in blue color model. When the excitation 365 nm UV light was turned off, however, the emission immediately changes to blue-green RTP, which can be easily identified by scanning the barcode. Therefore, the RTP materials exhibiting dual-mode emission anti-counterfeiting properties. In addition, a hidden security information “2019” was written on the non-FL-background paper using the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite and interference CDs (Fig. 5b, reported CDs: with blue emissive fluorescence but without the RTP properties). Upon excitation with a 365 nm UV lamp, wrong information (the number “8888” with blue emissive FL) could be readily observed. However, the encryption feature “2019” only becomes clearly seen under the phosphorescent mode after switching off the UV excitation. Thus, these observations demonstrate that the outstanding ultralong RTP materials can be utilized for effective information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

In summary, we first presented an effective approach to develop nitrogen-doped CDs with RTP through facile heating treatment. Then, a new class of nanocomposite with efficiency RTP emission in aqueous media were synthesized by N-CDs and MA. The systematic investigation demonstrates that the N-CDs/MA nanocomposite can form robust hydrogen-bonded networks, which not only enhance the rigidifies the C = O bonds of the nanocomposite but also hinder molecular motions for suppressing non-radiative transition of singlet and triplet excitons, resulting in the ultralong phosphorescence. In addition, the obtained nanocomposite suspension can be utilized to Fe3+ detection based on dual emission (FL and RTP) performance. Furthermore, the prepared RTP materials also show great potential in the fields of information encryption and anti-counterfeiting.

Minghui Zan: Project administration, Visualization, Writing – original draft. Shuai An: Investigation, Data curation. Mingzheng Jia: Validation, Data curation. Lei Cao: Methodology, Writing – review & editing. Li Li: Formal analysis. Mingfeng Ge: Methodology, Data curation. Zaihui Wu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing. Qian Mei: Supervision, Funding acquisition. Wen-Fei Dong: Supervision, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was funded by the National Key R&D Program of China （Grand No. 2016YFF0103800), the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grand No. 81771982, 61535010, 8160071152, 21803075, and 81902166), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20181236and BK20170389), the Primary Research & Development Plan of Jiangsu Province (BE2019683), and the Science and Technology Department of Jinan City (2018GXRC016).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106878.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The influence of the molecular packing on the room temperature phosphorescence of purely organic luminogens

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Yang, X. Zhen, B. Wang, X. Gao, Z. Ren, J. Wang, Y. Xie, J. Li, Q. Peng, K. Pu, Z. Li, The influence of the molecular packing on the room temperature phosphorescence of purely organic luminogens, Nature Communications 9(1) (2018) 840.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Nano-photosensitizer based on layered double hydroxide and isophthalic acid for singlet oxygenation and photodynamic therapy

sb:title.sb:maintitle: The in vivo activation of persistent nanophosphors for optical imaging of vascularization, tumours and grafted cells

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: T. Maldiney, A. Bessière, J. Seguin, E. Teston, S.K. Sharma, B. Viana, A.J.J. Bos, P. Dorenbos, M. Bessodes, D. Gourier, D. Scherman, C. Richard, The in vivo activation of persistent nanophosphors for optical imaging of vascularization, tumours and grafted cells, Nature Materials 13 (2014) 418.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Phosphorescent polymeric thermometers for in vitro and in vivo temperature sensing with minimized background interference

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Chen, K.Y. Zhang, X. Tong, Y. Liu, C. Hu, S. Liu, Q. Yu, Q. Zhao, W. Huang, Phosphorescent Polymeric Thermometers for In Vitro and In Vivo Temperature Sensing with Minimized Background Interference, Advanced Functional Materials 26(24) (2016) 4386-4396.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Stabilizing triplet excited states for ultralong organic phosphorescence

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. An, C. Zheng, Y. Tao, R. Chen, H. Shi, T. Chen, Z. Wang, H. Li, R. Deng, X. Liu, W. Huang, Stabilizing triplet excited states for ultralong organic phosphorescence, Nature Materials 14 (2015) 685.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrogen-bonded two-component ionic crystals showing enhanced long-lived room-temperature phosphorescence via TADF-assisted förster resonance energy transfer

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: B. Zhou, D. Yan, Hydrogen-Bonded Two-Component Ionic Crystals Showing Enhanced Long-Lived Room-Temperature Phosphorescence via TADF-Assisted Förster Resonance Energy Transfer, Advanced Functional Materials 29(4) (2019) 1807599.
S.N. Baker, G.A. Baker, Luminescent Carbon Nanodots: Emergent Nanolights, Angewandte Chemie International Edition 49(38) (2010) 6726-6744.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tailoring intermolecular interactions for efficient room-temperature phosphorescence from purely organic materials in amorphous polymer matrices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M.S. Kwon, D. Lee, S. Seo, J. Jung, J. Kim, Tailoring Intermolecular Interactions for Efficient Room-Temperature Phosphorescence from Purely Organic Materials in Amorphous Polymer Matrices, Angewandte Chemie International Edition 53(42) (2014) 11177-11181.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Highly efficient orange and red phosphorescent organic light-emitting diodes with low roll-off of efficiency using a novel thermally activated delayed fluorescence material as host

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: H. Wang, L. Meng, X. Shen, X. Wei, X. Zheng, X. Lv, Y. Yi, Y. Wang, P. Wang, Highly Efficient Orange and Red Phosphorescent Organic Light-Emitting Diodes with Low Roll-Off of Efficiency using a Novel Thermally Activated Delayed Fluorescence Material as Host, Advanced Materials 27(27) (2015) 4041-4047.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progresses on materials for electrophosphorescent organic light-emitting devices

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Xiao, Z. Chen, B. Qu, J. Luo, S. Kong, Q. Gong, J. Kido, Recent Progresses on Materials for Electrophosphorescent Organic Light-Emitting Devices, Advanced Materials 23(8) (2011) 926-952.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Direct observation of triplet state emission of single molecules: single molecule phosphorescence quenching of metalloporphyrin and organometallic complexes by molecular oxygen and their quenching rate distributions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: E. Mei, S. Vinogradov, R.M. Hochstrasser, Direct Observation of Triplet State Emission of Single Molecules: Single Molecule Phosphorescence Quenching of Metalloporphyrin and Organometallic Complexes by Molecular Oxygen and Their Quenching Rate Distributions, Journal of the American Chemical Society 125(43) (2003) 13198-13204.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Activating efficient phosphorescence from purely organic materials by crystal design
